---
title: "Ultrasound Alveolar to Dental L1 English L2 Spanish"
shorttitle: "Ultrasound: Alveolar to Dental"
author:
  - name: Robert Esposito
    corresponding: true
    email: rme70@rutgers.edu
    affiliations:
      name: "Rutgers University"
      department: Department of Spanish and Portuguese
      address: 15 Seminary Pl.
      city: New Brunswick
      region: NJ
      country: USA
      postal-code: 08901
author-note:
  # Disclosures condensed to one paragraph, but you can start a field with two line breaks to break them up: \n\nNew Paragraph
  disclosures:
    # Example: This study was registered at X (Identifier Y).
    study-registration: ~
    # Acknowledge and cite data/materials to be shared.
    data-sharing: ~
    # Example: This article is based on data published in [Reference].
    # Example: This article is based on the dissertation completed by [citation].  
    related-report: ~
    # Example: [Author name] has been a paid consultant for Corporation X, which funded this study.
    conflict-of-interest: The authors have no conflicts of interest to disclose.
    # Example: This study was supported by Grant [Grant Number] from [Funding Source].
    financial-support: ~
    # Example: The authors are grateful to [Person] for [Reason].
    gratitude: ~
    # Example. Because the authors are equal contributors, order of authorship was determined by a fair coin toss.
    authorship-agreements: ~
number-sections: false
abstract: "THE ABSTRACT GOES HERE."
keywords: [the, keywords, go, here]
floatsintext: true
bibliography: "./lit/l2_esp_alv_to_dent.bib"
format:
  apaquarto-docx: default
  apaquarto-html: default
  apaquarto-pdf:
    documentmode: man
---

```{r}
#| include: false

# load libs
library(here)
```

Second language (L2) learners have the task of acquiring a new phonological system, including language-specific timing and fine-grained phonetic details, after they have already established their first language (L1). Although some researchers have claimed that native-like acquisition of fine-grained phonetics is impossible [@scovel1988power; @long1990least], others have demonstrated that exceptional cases exist in which L2 speakers have performed in a native-like manner [@colantoni2006native; @face2021ultimate]. In the L2 classroom, phonetics receives little attention in general [@derwing2005second; @gilbert2014intonation] despite the fact that both explicit [@gordon2012pronunciation] and implicit [@saito2015communicative] teaching methodologies have resulted in gains for the L2 learners.

One methodology for incorporating training of non-native sounds in L2 learners is ultrasound visualization. Although ultrasound has of history of use in clinical settings for diagnosis and rehabilitation [@dugan2023qualitative], it has been extended to teaching spoken language to the deaf and hard of hearing [@bernhardt2005ultrasound; @bernhardt2003speech] and to L2 learners [@bliss2018computer; @gick2008ultrasound; @wilson2014using; @levitt2007augmented]. The present study is a single-participant design [@gick2008ultrasound] investigating the effects of ultrasound visualization feedback on the acquisition of /t d/ + rhotic clusters in L1 English L2 Spanish learners. Participants completed a pre-test, an intervention, and a post-test. Acoustic measures were collected during the pre- and post-tests as quantitative measures.

## Spanish vs English alveolar plosives acoustics and articulations

## L1 English L2 Spanish word-initial /t d/ acquisition

## L1 English L2 Spanish /ɾ/ acquisition

## L1 English L2 Spanish /tɾ dɾ/ acquisition

## Ultrasound

In general and for L2 feedback

# Method

## Participants

## Measures

## Procedure

# Results

# Discussion

## Limitations and Future Directions

## Conclusion

# References

<!-- References will auto-populate in the refs div below -->

::: {#refs}
:::
